text: The Stoic philosopher Seneca, writing to his friend Lucilius around 65 CE, described a man who could not bear his own company: 'He rushes from one thing to another, always in motion, never resting — not because he desires what he seeks, but because he fears what he leaves behind.' Seneca diagnosed this as a failure of philosophy: the untrained mind has never learned to inhabit itself. For the Stoics, focus was not a productivity strategy but a form of courage — the willingness to remain present with one's own thoughts rather than outsourcing awareness to spectacle.
question: According to Seneca, what drives the person who rushes constantly from one thing to another?

options.text: Fear of what he leaves behind, not genuine desire for what he seeks
A philosophical curiosity about the world and its many experiences
An ambition to accomplish as much as possible before death
A temperamental excess of energy that philosophy cannot cure
